neighborhoods_visited: Piazza del Duomo / Piazza della Repubblica
Piazza della Signoria / Uffizi / Ponte Vecchio
guide_description: Five days building a coherent picture of Florence: the cathedral complex and civic core, the gallery district around the Uffizi, the artisan quarter across the Arno, the Medici cultural corridor in the north, and a hilltop day trip to Fiesole with views over the entire valley. Each cluster is self-contained and walkable.

days.cluster_tagline: Florence's marble crown - the Duomo, Baptistery, and the bell tower that defines the skyline
days.neighborhood_focus: Piazza del Duomo / Piazza della Repubblica
days.neighborhood_description: The religious and commercial heart of medieval Florence, packed into a few hundred meters. The cathedral complex occupies the northern end; the Roman-grid market district around Piazza della Repubblica fills the space where the ancient forum once stood. Almost everything here is medieval or Renaissance fabric built on Roman foundations.

morning.notes: Start here before the cathedral, since the Baptistery is often less crowded at opening. The Gates of Paradise on the east door are Ghiberti replicas - the originals are in the Museo dell'Opera del Duomo across the square, which is worth a separate visit. Inside, the 13th-century mosaic ceiling depicting the Last Judgment covers 1,200 square meters and predates the cathedral itself.

afternoon.activity_title: Florence Cathedral - Santa Maria del Fiore
afternoon.notes: Book the dome climb (Brunelleschi's cupola) on the Duomo complex website well ahead - it sells out days in advance, especially the first slot at 8:15am when the light inside the lantern is clearest and the queue at the top is thinnest. The climb is 463 steps with no lift; the narrow passage between the inner and outer shells is the architectural revelation, not just the view from the top. Cathedral floor entry is free but also requires a reservation.

evening.notes: The 414-step climb up the campanile gives the only view in Florence where Brunelleschi's dome fills the entire frame at close range - no other viewpoint gets this angle because everywhere else you are too far away or the dome is behind you. Late afternoon light hits the white, green, and pink marble polychrome facade at its best. The upper terrace is exposed and breezy; bring a layer.

if_time_permits.notes: The best rainy-day move in this cluster. Houses the original Ghiberti Gates of Paradise panels, Donatello's wooden Mary Magdalene - raw and unsettling in contrast to the marble smoothness everywhere else - and Michelangelo's late Pieta, which he carved for his own tomb and then mutilated in frustration. Included in the Duomo complex ticket.

Built in the 1880s by demolishing the old ghetto and medieval market. The triumphal arch inscription boasting of ancient squalor redeemed by new grandeur is one of the more honest pieces of urban self-congratulation in Italy. The historic caffes under the arcade - Gilli, Paszkowski, Giubbe Rosse - were the meeting ground for the Futurist movement; Gilli has been open since 1733.

days.cluster_tagline: The civic and artistic core - the seat of Florentine power and the world's oldest art gallery
days.neighborhood_focus: Piazza della Signoria / Uffizi / Ponte Vecchio
days.neighborhood_description: The political spine of Renaissance Florence running from the Palazzo Vecchio south through the Uffizi corridor to the Arno. The Medici built the Uffizi as government offices and later filled it with their art collection; the walkable distance from the civic square to the river is less than 400 meters.

morning.notes: Book timed entry online weeks in advance - the Uffizi is one of the most visited museums in Europe and walk-up queues in high season exceed two hours. The first-floor rooms covering the 13th and 14th centuries (Cimabue, Duccio, Giotto) are often rushed by visitors heading for Botticelli; they set up everything that follows. The Botticelli hall (room 10-14) is the main event - arrive at opening to see the Birth of Venus and Primavera without the crowd pressed against the rope.

afternoon.notes: The outdoor sculpture collection under the Loggia dei Lanzi is free and includes originals by Cellini (Perseus with the Head of Medusa) and Giambologna (Rape of the Sabine Women) - world-class bronzes standing in open air with no ticket required. The David in front of the Palazzo Vecchio is a copy; the original is at the Accademia. Savonarola was burned at the stake in this square in 1498; a circular marble plaque in the pavement marks the spot.

evening.notes: The only bridge in Florence the retreating German army declined to destroy in 1944 - Hitler reportedly gave a personal order to spare it. The goldsmiths replaced the medieval butchers and tanners by Medici decree in 1593; Ferdinand I considered the smell of meat incompatible with the Vasari Corridor running overhead. For the best exterior view, walk to the Ponte Santa Trinita one block west - the Vecchio frames perfectly from there at golden hour.

if_time_permits.notes: The Salone dei Cinquecento on the first floor is a vast hall painted by Vasari with battle scenes concealing - possibly - a lost Leonardo beneath. The secret passages (percorsi segreti) require a separate timed ticket and wind through hidden staircases behind the painted walls. Good afternoon addition if you finish the Uffizi before lunch.

Destroyed in 1944 and rebuilt stone by stone using original material pulled from the Arno. The four allegorical statues at the corners were recovered separately - the head of Spring was found only in 1961. The elliptical arch profile, credited to Michelangelo's influence on Ammannati, is still considered one of the most elegant bridge designs ever built.

days.cluster_tagline: Florence across the Arno - the artisan quarter, the Medici palace, and the best hill views in the city
days.neighborhood_description: The south bank of the Arno has always been the working-class counterpart to the merchant north. Palazzo Pitti anchors the formal end; west toward San Frediano the streets narrow into a still-active craft district of bookbinders, gilders, and leather workers. The hill above holds two of the finest viewpoints in Tuscany.

morning.notes: The Palatine Gallery on the second floor contains the Medici's personal art collection still hung as they left it - paintings stacked to the ceiling in period arrangement rather than the sparse museum spacing of the Uffizi. Raphael's La Velata and Titian's Portrait of a Gentleman are here without the crowds those works would attract elsewhere. The Boboli Gardens behind the palace are a 16th-century Mannerist landscape - the Neptune fountain at the upper terrace gives a first preview of the city roofline.

afternoon.notes: The standard panorama postcard of Florence - Duomo, Palazzo Vecchio, and the Arno bend in a single frame - is taken from here. Midday is the least rewarding time; the light is flat and the tour buses pack the square. Arrive late afternoon when the light comes from the west and the dome glows orange. Walk up via the stepped Viale Giuseppe Poggi path rather than the road - it passes through a rose garden that bypasses the worst of the coach-tour arrival zone.

evening.notes: The finest Romanesque church in Tuscany, begun in 1013, with a facade of white and green marble that predates the Duomo's exterior by three centuries. The crypt below the raised choir holds an 11th-century fresco cycle that almost no visitors see because the main body of the church draws all attention. Gregorian vespers are sung by the resident Benedictine monks most evenings around 5:30pm - the timing varies, but the door at the right side of the nave usually indicates whether a service is underway.

if_time_permits.notes: The frescoes by Masaccio and Masolino in this small chapel inside Santa Maria del Carmine are among the founding documents of Western painting - Masaccio's Expulsion from Eden (the couple's faces, not the bodies) changed how Renaissance artists depicted human emotion. Entry is strictly timed and capped at 30 visitors; book online the same morning or the day before.

days.cluster_tagline: The Medici's private Florence - their parish church, their library, their convent, and Michelangelo's David
days.neighborhood_description: The northern quarter of the historic center where the Medici family concentrated their patronage - the parish church of San Lorenzo, the monastic complex of San Marco decorated entirely by Fra Angelico, and the Accademia where Michelangelo's David has stood since 1873. The Mercato Centrale nearby is the city's main covered food market.

morning.notes: Book timed entry online - lines without a reservation stretch around the block by 9am in season. The David is 5.17 meters tall and the scale only registers in person; photographs consistently fail to convey it. Walk past the unfinished Prisoners - four rough-hewn marble figures Michelangelo abandoned - before entering the Tribune rotunda. They read like the David is emerging from the same stone, which was probably intentional.

afternoon.notes: Fra Angelico lived as a Dominican friar in this convent and painted an Annunciation at the top of each monk's staircase, then individual frescoes in nearly every cell - the same subject repeated with tiny variations in composition and color across 44 rooms. This is one of the most quietly overwhelming art experiences in Florence and often half-empty compared to the Uffizi. Savonarola's cell, preserved intact, is in the same building.

evening.activity_title: Basilica di San Lorenzo and Medici Chapels
evening.notes: The Medici family parish church, designed by Brunelleschi and the purest example of early Renaissance architecture in the city. The Medici Chapels (separate entrance on the north side, Piazza Madonna degli Aldobrandini) house Michelangelo's New Sacristy with the tombs of Lorenzo and Giuliano - the figures of Night, Day, Dawn, and Dusk represent the passage of time draining away human glory. Michelangelo left Florence in 1534 before completing the project and never returned.

if_time_permits.notes: Michelangelo designed the vestibule staircase and reading room for Pope Clement VII starting in 1523. The ricetto entrance hall is considered his first overtly Mannerist architectural work - columns set into niches rather than projecting from walls, a deliberate subversion of classical rules. The reading room contains the desks and benches Michelangelo designed, unchanged. Entry is through the San Lorenzo cloister.

The 1874 cast-iron market hall has ground-floor stalls with cheese, salumi, bread, and produce - best visited before noon when vendors are most active. The upper floor is a food court that gets crowded at lunch but the lampredotto (tripe) stands at street level outside the south entrance are the more authentic Florentine option.

days.cluster_tagline: The Etruscan hill town above Florence - Roman ruins, monastery gardens, and the whole Arno valley laid out below
days.neighborhood_description: A small hilltop town 8 kilometers northeast of Florence, older than the city below it. Fiesole was an Etruscan and then Roman settlement from which the Arno valley city was visible long before Florence existed. A Roman theatre, a cathedral older than the Duomo, and a Franciscan convent sit within a few hundred meters of each other on the ridge.

morning.notes: A first-century Roman theatre still used for summer concerts, a Roman bathhouse complex, an Etruscan temple platform, and a small museum are all contained within one ticket. The theatre is one of the best-preserved in northern Italy and the site is rarely crowded even in high season. Come in the morning when the light falls into the theatre from the east. Bus 7 from Florence Santa Maria Novella station runs every 20-30 minutes and terminates in Fiesole's main piazza, a 5-minute walk downhill to the site.

afternoon.notes: Founded in 1028, predating Florence Cathedral by over two centuries. The interior is plain Romanesque - a deliberate contrast to the marble polychrome of everything in Florence below. The crypt holds the remains of Saint Romulus and a Mino da Fiesole altar piece that is regularly overlooked because the town's main attractions pull visitors toward the archaeological zone. Take five minutes in the crypt.

evening.activity_title: Franciscan Convent of Fiesole and viewpoint
evening.notes: The convent occupies the summit of the Fiesole hill at 346 meters and the terrace in front of the church gives a 180-degree view from the Apennines across the entire Arno valley with Florence spread below. The view from here is the one that appears in the background of so many Quattrocento panel paintings - the painters were depicting what they actually saw from this hill. The convent church itself is tiny and worth a brief look for the Franciscan simplicity after days of Medici excess.

if_time_permits.notes: The 15th-century Medici villa on the slope below Fiesole - the first villa designed specifically to exploit a hillside view rather than being a fortified country house. Lorenzo de' Medici held Platonic Academy meetings here. The terraced gardens are open for visits and the belvedere terrace looks directly down onto Florence with a framing of cypress and olive that makes it the most painterly of the Fiesole viewpoints.
